parsed_args.prompt: Describe the two distinct voting mechanisms for electing directors, including the Class B shareholders voting as a class to elect a majority of directors, and the combined Class A and Class B voting power for remaining board seats. Include all details about board composition and voting rights in {{spacex_s1}}.

tool_output.output:
Based on the provided text, here is a detailed description of the two distinct voting mechanisms for electing directors, along with the associated details about board composition and voting rights.

The company's charter establishes a dual-class stock structure that creates two separate paths for electing the board of directors.

### The Two Voting Mechanisms for Electing Directors

**1. Class B Directors (Majority of the Board)**
The first mechanism grants the holders of Class B common stock the exclusive right to elect a majority of the board. The charter specifies that:
*   **Electing Body:** Holders of Class B common stock, **voting separately as a class**.
*   **Number of Directors:** They are entitled to elect **51% of the total number of authorized directors**, rounded up to the nearest whole number.
*   **Duration:** This right exists **for so long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding**.
*   **Terminology:** Directors elected through this mechanism are referred to as the **“Class B Directors.”**

**2. Common Stock Directors (Remaining Board Seats)**
The second mechanism is used to fill the remaining board seats not elected by the Class B shareholders. The charter specifies that:
*   **Electing Body:** Holders of **all classes of voting common stock (Class A and Class B), voting together as a single class**.
*   **Number of Directors:** They are entitled to elect the **remaining directors** after the Class B directors are seated.
*   **Terminology:** Directors elected through this combined vote are referred to as the **“Common Stock Directors.”**

### Board Composition, Voting Rights, and Related Procedures

The text provides further details on how these two classes of directors are controlled after their election.

*   **Board Size and Practical Control:** Upon the completion of the offering, the board will consist of **eight directors**. The documents state that Elon Musk, through his majority ownership of Class B shares, will be able to elect all the Class B Directors. Furthermore, because he will also hold a majority of the combined voting power of Class A and Class B stock, he will be able to elect the Common Stock Directors as well, giving him the power to control the election of the entire board.

*   **Removal Procedures:**
    *   **Class B Directors:** Can be removed with or without cause by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding Class B common stock, voting separately as a class.
    *   **Common Stock Directors:** Can be removed with or without cause by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding voting common stock (Class A and B), voting together as a single class.

*   **Vacancy Filling:**
    *   **Class B Director Vacancies:** May be filled by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, **or** by the remaining Class B Directors, and not by any other persons.
    *   **Common Stock Director Vacancies:** May be filled by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding voting common stock, voting together as a single class, **or** by the remaining directors.

*   **Special Leadership Protections:** The charter provides a special voting right specifically for Elon Musk's leadership roles. His removal from the board and from his positions as **Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of the board** requires the approval of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding Class B common stock, voting separately as a class.

*   **Other Class B Voting Rights:** Beyond director elections, the approval of the Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, is required for any amendment to the charter that would change the rights, powers, preferences, and privileges of the Class B common stock (including with respect to Class B Directors).

*   **Preferred Stock Precedence:** All voting rights described are subject to the rights that may be granted in the future to holders of any series of preferred stock.

parsed_args.prompt: Describe the capital structure, including Class A, Class B, and Class C shares, their respective voting rights (votes per share), and any other key differences between share classes in {{spacex_s1}}.

tool_output.output:
Based on the provided document chunks, here is a description of the capital structure for Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (SpaceX), focusing on the Class A, Class B, and Class C common stock.

### Authorized Shares (Post-IPO)

Upon completion of the offering, the authorized capital stock will include:
*   **Class A common stock:** 36,132,150,000 shares, par value $0.001 per share.
*   **Class B common stock:** 6,125,000,000 shares, par value $0.001 per share.
*   **Class C common stock:** 10,000,000,000 shares, par value $0.001 per share. No shares of Class C will be issued and outstanding immediately after the offering.
*   **Preferred stock:** 2,400,000,000 shares, par value $0.001 per share. No shares will be issued and outstanding immediately after the offering.

### Voting Rights (Votes Per Share)

*   **Class A common stock:** Entitled to **one vote per share**.
*   **Class B common stock:** Entitled to **ten votes per share**.
*   **Class C common stock:** Holders have **no voting rights**, except as required by law.

Generally, holders of Class A and Class B common stock vote together as a single class on all matters submitted to shareholders, except as specifically provided in the charter.

### Key Differences Between Share Classes

The primary differences between the share classes are in **voting power and board control**, not in economic rights.

**1. Board of Directors Election Control:**
*   Holders of **Class B common stock**, voting separately as a class, are entitled to elect **51% of the total number of authorized directors** (rounded up to the nearest whole number). This right exists for as long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding.
*   The holders of all classes of voting common stock (Class A and Class B), voting together as a single class, elect the remaining directors.

**2. Protective Provisions for Class B Common Stock:**
*   **Removal of Elon Musk:** The removal of Elon Musk from his roles as Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of the board requires the approval of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding shares of Class B common stock, voting separately as a class.
*   **Removal and Vacancies of Class B Directors:** Class B Directors may only be removed by a majority vote of the outstanding Class B common stock. Vacancies among the Class B Directors may only be filled by a majority vote of the outstanding Class B common stock or by the remaining Class B Directors.
*   **Charter Amendments and Major Transactions:** The approval of the Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, is required to amend the charter in a way that changes the rights, powers, or preferences of the Class B common stock, and to approve certain combinations, mergers, or sales as described in the charter.

**3. Conversion Rights:**
*   **Class B Common Stock:** Each share of Class B common stock is convertible at any time at the option of the holder into **one share of Class A common stock**.
*   **Automatic Conversion of Class B Common Stock:** Each share of Class B common stock will automatically convert into one share of Class A common stock upon a "Transfer" of that share, whether or not for value, subject to exceptions for certain "Permitted Transfers" (e.g., transfers to family members, trusts, and certain related entities for tax or estate planning purposes).
*   **Class A and Class C Common Stock:** Holders of Class A and Class C common stock do not have conversion rights.

**4. Economic Rights (Dividends & Liquidation - Equal):**
*   **Dividends:** Holders of Class A, Class B, and Class C common stock are entitled to receive dividends on a pro rata, equal priority, pari passu basis when declared by the board.
*   **Liquidation:** Upon liquidation, dissolution, or winding up, holders of Class A, Class B, and Class C common stock share ratably in all remaining assets after payments to creditors and any liquidation preferences of outstanding preferred stock.
